timeline.event: Helen Adams Keller was born in a house at Ivy Green in Tuscumbia, Alabama, USA.
Helen's mother, Kate Keller, was inspired by reading Charles Dickens' 'American Notes' and went to Baltimore to seek advice from J. Julian Chisolm.
Anne Sullivan arrived at Helen's house and began to teach her.
Helen enrolled in the Perkins School for the Blind.
Wrote 'The Frost King' at the age of 11.
Helen Keller and Anne Sullivan moved to New York to attend the Wright-Humason School for the Deaf and the Horace Mann School for the Deaf.
Returned to Massachusetts and attended The Cambridge School for Young Ladies.
Admitted to Radcliffe College, the women's college affiliated with Harvard University.
Published her autobiography 'The Story of My Life' with Anne Sullivan and Sullivan's husband, John Macy.
Graduated from Radcliffe College at the age of 24, becoming the first deafblind person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree.
Wrote 'The World I Live In'.
Joined the Socialist Party of America at the age of 29.
Joined the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).
Published 'Out of the Dark', a series of essays on socialism.
Founded an organization called 'Helen Keller International' with George Kessler.
Wrote articles for the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).
Appeared in the silent film 'Deliverance'.
Helped found the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU).
Published her spiritual autobiography 'My Religion'.
Anne Sullivan died.
Asked about the loyal dog Hachiko during a visit to Akita Prefecture, Japan, and received an Akita dog named 'Kamikaze-go'.
When 'Kamikaze-go' died, she received his older brother 'Kenzan-go' as an official gift from the Japanese government.
William Gibson's script for Playhouse 90 was realized as the first production of 'The Miracle Worker'.
Polly Thomson died of a stroke.
Suffered a series of strokes and spent her final years at home.
The film 'The Miracle Worker' starring Anne Bancroft and Patty Duke won Academy Awards.
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from US President Lyndon B. Johnson.
Inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame at the New York World's Fair.
Died at the age of 88 at her home, Arcan Ridge, in Easton, Connecticut.
Helen Keller was selected as one of the 18 'most widely admired people of the 20th century' by Gallup.
The state of Alabama featured Helen Keller on its state quarter (25-cent coin).
A bronze statue of Helen Keller was added to the National Statuary Hall Collection.

keyAchievements.title: First deafblind person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree
keyAchievements.description: Graduated from Radcliffe College in 1904, becoming the first blind and deaf person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific author and educator
keyAchievements.description: Published a total of 12 books and numerous articles, and was active as an American author and educator.

keyAchievements.title: Socialist and human rights activist
keyAchievements.description: Actively participated in various social movements including women's suffrage, pacifism, abolition of the death penalty, and opposition to child labor and racism.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Helen Keller International
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded 'Helen Keller International' with George Kessler in 1915, dedicated to research in vision, health, and nutrition.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to the founding of the ACLU
keyAchievements.description: Helped found the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) in 1920.

keyAchievements.title: National honors
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1964, inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame in 1965, and honored in various ways posthumously.

faq.question: When was Helen Keller born and when did she die?
faq.answer: Helen Keller was born on June 27, 1880, and died on June 1, 1968, at the age of 88.

faq.question: How did Helen Keller lose her sight and hearing?
faq.answer: At 19 months of age, she was diagnosed with scarlet fever and meningitis, which left her with lifelong visual and hearing impairments.

faq.question: Who had the greatest influence on Helen Keller's education?
faq.answer: Teacher Anne Sullivan arrived at Helen's house in March 1887 and had a decisive influence on her life by teaching her language by spelling words into her hand.

faq.question: What social movements did Helen Keller participate in?
faq.answer: Helen Keller was a socialist intellectual who practiced various human rights movements, including women's suffrage, pacifism, abolition of the death penalty, and opposition to child labor and racism.

faq.question: What is the famous work about Helen Keller's life?
faq.answer: The film 'The Miracle Worker', which depicts Helen Keller's childhood and her relationship with Anne Sullivan, is the most well-known, and the 1962 film won Academy Awards.

timeline.event: Baptized at the Catholic Parish of St. Remigius in Bonn, Germany.
Promoted as a six-year-old 'child prodigy' on the poster for his first public concert.
Began taking lessons from Christian Gottlob Neefe in Bonn.
Began working as Neefe's assistant organist.
His first published work, the piano variations 'Nine Variations on a March by Ernst Christoph Dressler, WoO 63', was published.
Three Piano Sonatas, WoO 47, were published.
The first article about Beethoven appeared in the 'Magazin der Musik'.
Began working as a paid employee at the court chapel and was permanently appointed as the second court organist.
His father Johann was forced to retire from the court, and an order was issued that half of his pension be paid directly to Beethoven to support the family.
Believed to have been commissioned to compose the 'Cantata on the Death of Emperor Joseph II, WoO 87' and the 'Cantata on the Accession of Emperor Leopold II, WoO 88'.
Believed to have been first introduced to Joseph Haydn when Haydn stopped in Bonn during his trip to London.
Commissioned to compose the ballet music 'Musik zu einem Ritterballett, WoO 1'.
Met Haydn again in Bonn on Haydn's return trip from London to Vienna, and Haydn agreed to take Beethoven as a student.
Left Bonn for Vienna with the help of the Elector.
Learned of his father's death shortly after arriving in Vienna.
Established a reputation as a piano virtuoso in Vienna.
Bonn fell to the French.
Performed one of his piano concertos at his first public concert in Vienna.
Arranged for the publication of his first work assigned an opus number (Three Piano Trios Nos. 1-3, Op. 1).
Toured cultural centers in Central Europe, including Prague, Dresden, Leipzig, and Berlin.
Composed Cello Sonatas Nos. 1-2, Op. 5 in Berlin and dedicated them to King Frederick William II.
Returned to Vienna.
Traveled to Pressburg and Pest.
Suffered from severe illness every summer and autumn.
First noticed problems with his hearing.
As his hearing gradually weakened during a trip to Prague, he eventually gave up concert tours altogether.
Composed his first six String Quartets, Op. 18 at the request of Prince Joseph Franz Lobkowitz.
Won a piano 'duel' against the virtuoso Joseph Woelfl.
Piano Sonata No. 8 'Pathétique', Op. 13 was published.
Completed the Septet, Op. 20.
Began teaching piano to the daughters of the Hungarian noblewoman Anna Brunsvik.
Won a piano 'duel' against Daniel Steibelt.
Hired the Burgtheater for the premiere of his first symphony and staged an extensive program.
With the premiere of Symphonies Nos. 1 and 2, he came to be regarded as the most important of a young generation of composers.
The first six String Quartets, Op. 18 were published and dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz.
Completed the ballet music 'The Creatures of Prometheus, Op. 43' and premiered it at the Burgtheater in Vienna.
Met the young countess Giulietta Guicciardi.
Taught Ferdinand Ries.
Young Carl Czerny studied under Beethoven.
Published Piano Sonata No. 14, Op. 27 No. 2 ('Moonlight Sonata') and dedicated it to Giulietta Guicciardi.
Completed Symphony No. 2, but the concert was canceled.
Moved to Heiligenstadt just outside Vienna to try and come to terms with his condition, and wrote the Heiligenstadt Testament.
Began studying piano and composition with Archduke Rudolf of Austria.
Composed Symphony No. 3 'Eroica'.
Count Joseph Deym died.
His position at the Theater an der Wien ended due to a change in management.
Disillusioned by Napoleon declaring himself 'Emperor', he scratched Napoleon's name off the title page of the manuscript of Symphony No. 3.
Symphony No. 3 'Eroica' was premiered.
The opera Fidelio was premiered to nearly empty houses due to the French occupation of the city.
Symphony No. 3 'Eroica' was published.
Musician and publisher Muzio Clementi secured the rights to publish his works in Britain.
Prince Esterhazy commissioned a mass (Mass in C major, Op. 86) for his wife's name day.
Organized and premiered a massive benefit concert that included Symphonies Nos. 5 and 6, Piano Concerto No. 4, the Mass in C major, and the Choral Fantasy.
Was offered the position of Kapellmeister at the court of Kassel by Jerome Bonaparte, leading Archduke Rudolf, Count Kinsky, and Prince Lobkowitz to pledge him a pension to stay in Vienna.
Commissioned to compose incidental music for Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's play Egmont.
The incidental music for Egmont (Op. 84) appeared.
Proposed marriage to Therese Malfatti but was rejected.
Prince Lobkowitz suspended his pension payments.
Fell seriously ill with headaches and high fever.
Met Goethe in Teplitz.
While in Teplitz, he wrote a 10-page love letter to his 'Immortal Beloved'.
Visited his younger brother Johann, hoping to end his cohabitation with Therese Obermayer.
His brother Johann and Therese Obermayer were married.
Prince Kinsky died after falling from a horse.
Went through a difficult emotional period and his compositional output dropped.
Lent 1,500 florins to his brother Kaspar.
Motivated to start composing by the news of Napoleon's defeat at the Battle of Vitoria by the coalition led by the Duke of Wellington.
Wellington's Victory (Op. 91) and his Seventh Symphony, Op. 92 were first performed at a charity concert for war victims.
Wellington's Victory was received with repeated performances.
Beethoven made his final public appearance as a soloist playing in the Archduke Trio.
Began using an ear trumpet designed by Maelzel.
Produced music with a patriotic tone to entertain the many heads of state and diplomats attending the Congress of Vienna.
Received compensation from Prince Lobkowitz by resorting to the law.
Composed 'Calm Sea and Prosperous Voyage', Op. 112, for choir and orchestra based on poems by Goethe.
After his brother Kaspar died, he became embroiled in a long legal battle with Kaspar's wife Johanna over the custody of his nephew Karl.
Beethoven's output dropped to a level unequaled in his mature life.
Succeeded in taking his nephew Karl away from his mother's custody.
Completed 'To the Distant Beloved', Op. 98.
Suffered from a prolonged illness ('inflammatory fever').
Sought and obtained Beethoven's endorsement for Maelzel's newly developed metronome.
Beethoven's health improved, and his eleven-year-old nephew Karl moved in with him in January.
Completed Piano Sonata No. 29 'Hammerklavier', Op. 106.
Began musical sketches that would become part of his final Symphony No. 9.
Lost sole guardianship when the case was moved to the Vienna magistracy.
It was announced that Archduke Rudolf was promoted to Cardinal-Archbishop.
Publisher Anton Diabelli invited 50 Viennese composers, including Beethoven, to compose a variation.
Completed twenty of what would become the thirty-three Diabelli Variations, Op. 120.
Publisher Moritz Schlesinger visited Beethoven for the first time.
Regained custody of his nephew Karl after a fierce legal battle.
Rudolf's installation in Olomouc (triggered the Missa solemnis, Op. 123).
Piano Sonata No. 30, Op. 109 was dispatched to the publisher.
Suffered from rheumatism and jaundice, showing poor health once again.
In a letter to Rudolf, Beethoven revealed his belief in a personal God.
Piano Sonata No. 31, Op. 110 was published.
Wished to reconcile with his married brother Johann.
'Calm Sea and Prosperous Voyage', Op. 112 was published with a dedication to the poet Goethe.
The Philharmonic Society of London proposed a commission for a symphony.
Prince Nikolai Galitzin requested three string quartets from Beethoven.
Anton Schindler began working as the composer's unpaid secretary.
Missa solemnis, Symphony No. 9, and Diabelli Variations were completed.
Presented a copy of the completed Mass to Rudolf.
Symphony No. 9 and sections of the Missa solemnis were performed for the first time at the Kärntnertortheater to great acclaim.
The second performance, for which the producer guaranteed a minimum fee, had poor attendance.
String Quartet No. 12 in E-flat major, Op. 127 was premiered by the Schuppanzigh Quartet.
Suffered a sudden illness while writing Quartet No. 15 in A minor, Op. 132.
The final contrapuntal movement of Quartet No. 13 in B-flat major, Op. 130 proved very difficult for both performers and audience at its premiere.
Despite reconciling with Schindler, his secretary was replaced by Karl Holz.
Nephew Karl, who had been seeing his mother again, attempted suicide.
Moved to his brother Johann's house in Gneixendorf with his nephew and stayed for three months.
Completed the substitute finale for Quartet No. 13 in B-flat major, Op. 130, his final work.
Caught pneumonia on the return journey to Vienna in an open carriage in cold and damp weather.
His nephew Karl left to join the army in Jihlava.
Said to Schindler and others, 'Applaud, friends, the comedy is over.'
Died at the age of 56.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a composer and pianist
keyAchievements.description: A German composer of Western classical music and pianist, he is a major musician who was active during the transition between the Classical and Romantic eras.

keyAchievements.title: Moniker of 'Saint of Music'
keyAchievements.description: Often depicted as a heroic figure, he is called by the moniker 'Saint of Music' (Akseong).

keyAchievements.title: Major representative works
keyAchievements.description: His most widely known works include Symphony No. 3, Symphony No. 5, Symphony No. 6, Symphony No. 9, Pathétique Sonata, and Moonlight Sonata.

keyAchievements.title: First published work
keyAchievements.description: In March 1783, he published his first work, the piano variations 'Nine Variations on a March by Ernst Christoph Dressler, WoO 63'.

keyAchievements.title: Early piano sonatas
keyAchievements.description: The Three Piano Sonatas, WoO 47, published in 1783, were praised for giving an early glimpse of his talent and boldness as a composer.

keyAchievements.title: Ballet music commission
keyAchievements.description: In 1791, he was commissioned to compose the ballet music 'Musik zu einem Ritterballett, WoO 1', which was Beethoven's first work for the stage.

keyAchievements.title: First Opus work
keyAchievements.description: In 1795, he achieved financial success by publishing his first work assigned an opus number (Three Piano Trios Nos. 1-3, Op. 1).

keyAchievements.title: Cello Sonatas Nos. 1-2, Op. 5
keyAchievements.description: Composed in Berlin in 1796, they are famous for carefully considering the different characters of the cello and piano and combining the virtuoso parts well.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 8 'Pathétique', Op. 13
keyAchievements.description: Published in 1799, this work was described as surpassing any of his previous works 'in strength of character, depth of emotion, level of originality, and ingenuity of motif and tone'.

keyAchievements.title: Septet, Op. 20
keyAchievements.description: Completed in 1799, it was one of his most popular works during his lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: First six String Quartets, Op. 18
keyAchievements.description: Composed between 1798 and 1800, they were published in 1801 and dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz.

keyAchievements.title: Symphonies Nos. 1 and 2
keyAchievements.description: Premiered in 1800 and 1803, they led him to be regarded as the most important of the young generation of composers after Haydn and Mozart.

keyAchievements.title: Ballet music 'The Creatures of Prometheus, Op. 43'
keyAchievements.description: Completed in the spring of 1801, it premiered at the Burgtheater in Vienna and achieved success.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 14 'Moonlight Sonata', Op. 27 No. 2
keyAchievements.description: Published in 1802 and dedicated to Giulietta Guicciardi.

keyAchievements.title: Symphony No. 3 'Eroica'
keyAchievements.description: Composed in 1803-04, it is a major work that marks the beginning of his middle or 'heroic' period.

keyAchievements.title: Opera Fidelio
keyAchievements.description: Premiered in November 1805, it was later completed through revisions.

keyAchievements.title: Mass in C major, Op. 86
keyAchievements.description: Composed in 1807 commissioned by Prince Esterhazy.

keyAchievements.title: Massive benefit concert
keyAchievements.description: In December 1808, he organized and premiered a massive benefit concert that included Symphonies Nos. 5 and 6, Piano Concerto No. 4, the Mass in C major, and the Choral Fantasy.

keyAchievements.title: Incidental music for Goethe's play Egmont, Op. 84
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned in late 1809 and appeared in 1810, it fit well into Beethoven's 'heroic' style.

keyAchievements.title: String Quartet No. 11 in F minor, Op. 95 ('Quartetto Serioso')
keyAchievements.description: Known as another work from this period.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Trio No. 7 in B-flat major, Op. 97 ('Archduke Trio')
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to his patron Rudolf.

keyAchievements.title: Wellington's Victory, Op. 91 and Symphony No. 7, Op. 92
keyAchievements.description: First performed at a charity concert on December 8, 1813, achieving great success.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 27 in E minor, Op. 90
keyAchievements.description: His first piano sonata in five years, composed in the summer of 1814.

keyAchievements.title: To the Distant Beloved, Op. 98
keyAchievements.description: Completed in 1816, it introduced the song cycle into the classical repertoire.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 29 'Hammerklavier', Op. 106
keyAchievements.description: One of the major works completed in 1818.

keyAchievements.title: Missa solemnis, Op. 123
keyAchievements.description: Triggered by the announcement of Archduke Rudolf's promotion in 1819, it was completed in 1823.

keyAchievements.title: Thirty-three Diabelli Variations, Op. 120
keyAchievements.description: Started at the request of Antonio Diabelli in 1819 and completed in 1823.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonatas No. 30, Op. 109 and No. 31, Op. 110
keyAchievements.description: Some of his final piano sonatas, published in 1820 and 1821 respectively.

keyAchievements.title: Symphony No. 9
keyAchievements.description: Starting sketches in 1818, it was completed in 1823 and premiered on May 7, 1824, receiving praise that 'the inexhaustible genius has shown us a new world'.

keyAchievements.title: String Quartet No. 12 in E-flat major, Op. 127
keyAchievements.description: Premiered by the Schuppanzigh Quartet in March 1825.

keyAchievements.title: Quartet No. 14 in C-sharp minor, Op. 131
keyAchievements.description: The final part of the series, evaluated by Beethoven as his most perfect single work.

keyAchievements.title: Quartet No. 16 in F major, Op. 135
keyAchievements.description: An additional quartet completed while staying in Gneixendorf.

faq.question: When and where was Beethoven born?
faq.answer: He was born in Bonn, Germany, and there is a record of his baptism on December 17, 1770. Since it was customary at the time to baptize within 24 hours of birth, Beethoven himself considered December 16 as his birthday.

faq.question: What are Beethoven's major works?
faq.answer: His most widely known works include Symphony No. 3, Symphony No. 5, Symphony No. 6, Symphony No. 9, Pathétique Sonata, and Moonlight Sonata.

faq.question: When did Beethoven die, and what is suspected to be the cause of his death?
faq.answer: He died on March 26, 1827, at the age of 56. Liver cirrhosis, complications from pneumonia, and lead poisoning are suspected as causes of death.

faq.question: Did Beethoven completely lose his hearing?
faq.answer: Beethoven gradually lost his hearing, but even in his later years he could distinguish low tones and sudden loud noises, and did not become completely deaf.

faq.question: What is Beethoven's moniker?
faq.answer: He is often depicted as a heroic figure and is also called by the moniker 'Saint of Music' (Akseong).

faq.question: During which musical period was Beethoven active?
faq.answer: He is a major musician who was active during the transition between the Classical and Romantic eras.

timeline.event: Born in Hyde Park, New York.
His father, James Roosevelt I, died.
Graduated from Harvard University in 3 years.
Enrolled in Columbia Law School.
Married Eleanor Roosevelt.
Passed the New York bar exam and dropped out of Columbia Law School.
Elected to the New York State Senate.
Inaugurated as New York State Senator.
Re-elected to the New York State Senate.
Appointed Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Woodrow Wilson.
Eleanor Roosevelt discovered his affair with Lucy Mercer.
Ran as James M. Cox's vice presidential running mate but was defeated.
His lower body became paralyzed due to illness and was diagnosed with polio.
Established a rehabilitation center in Warm Springs, Georgia.
Elected Governor of New York.
The Wall Street Crash occurred and the Great Depression began.
Re-elected as Governor of New York.
Won the US presidential election against Herbert Hoover.
Survived an assassination attempt by Giuseppe Zangara.
Inaugurated as the 32nd President of the United States.
Declared a four-day 'bank holiday'.
The Emergency Banking Act was passed.
Signed the Cullen-Harrison Act, ending Prohibition.
Implemented the National Industrial Recovery Act (NIRA).
Reformed financial regulations through the Glass-Steagall Act.
Established the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).
Established the Federal Communications Commission (FCC).
The US Supreme Court declared the National Industrial Recovery Act unconstitutional.
The Social Security Act was passed.
Established the Works Progress Administration (WPA).
Re-elected in a landslide victory in the US presidential election.
Failed to pass the Judicial Procedures Reform Bill.
The Housing Act was passed.
Founded the National Foundation for Infantile Paralysis.
The Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) was passed.
Passed the Reorganization Act and established the Executive Office of the President.
World War II broke out with Germany's invasion of Poland.
Won the US presidential election, becoming the only president to serve more than three terms.
Delivered the 'Four Freedoms' speech.
Obtained congressional approval for the Lend-Lease Act.
Received a declaration of war from Japan following the attack on Pearl Harbor.
Germany and Italy declared war on the United States.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Longest-serving US President
keyAchievements.description: He is the only president in US history to serve the longest and complete more than three terms.

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of the New Deal
keyAchievements.description: Implemented the New Deal policies to overcome the Great Depression, providing relief to the unemployed and farmers, and seeking economic recovery.

keyAchievements.title: Political realignment and pioneering modern liberalism
keyAchievements.description: Built the 'New Deal Coalition', reorganizing US politics into the Fifth Party System and pioneering modern liberalism.

keyAchievements.title: Financial and labor reforms
keyAchievements.description: Introduced major regulatory reforms in finance, communications, and labor, such as establishing the SEC and the FDIC, and enacting the National Labor Relations Act.

keyAchievements.title: Repeal of Prohibition
keyAchievements.description: Signed the Cullen-Harrison Act to end the Prohibition era.

keyAchievements.title: Leadership during World War II
keyAchievements.description: Worked closely with other national leaders to lead the Allied Powers against the Axis Powers and oversaw the economic mobilization of the United States.

keyAchievements.title: Initiation of nuclear weapon development
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the development of the world's first nuclear weapons.

keyAchievements.title: Laying the foundation for the United Nations
keyAchievements.description: Cooperated with other Allied leaders to lay the foundation for the United Nations and other postwar institutions.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of the Good Neighbor Policy
keyAchievements.description: Promoted the Good Neighbor Policy, renouncing the right to unilaterally intervene in the affairs of Latin American countries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Social Security System
keyAchievements.description: Legally established the Social Security System through the Social Security Act, promising economic security to the elderly, the poor, and the disabled.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of labor standards
keyAchievements.description: Passed the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), outlawing child labor and establishing a federal minimum wage.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Executive Office of the President
keyAchievements.description: Established the Executive Office of the President, the backbone of the federal executive branch, through the Reorganization Act of 1939.

keyAchievements.title: Environmental conservation efforts
keyAchievements.description: Encouraged the expansion and funding of national parks and national forests and made environmental conservation efforts through the Civilian Conservation Corps.

faq.question: Which president was Franklin D. Roosevelt?
faq.answer: He served as the 32nd President of the United States.

faq.question: What are the major achievements of Franklin D. Roosevelt?
faq.answer: He implemented the New Deal to overcome the Great Depression and led the Allies in World War II.

faq.question: How many times was Franklin D. Roosevelt elected president?
faq.answer: He was elected president a total of 4 times, the only person in US history to serve more than 3 terms.

faq.question: What disease did Franklin D. Roosevelt suffer from?
faq.answer: He was diagnosed with polio in 1921, which left his lower body paralyzed.

faq.question: Who was Franklin D. Roosevelt's wife?
faq.answer: His wife was Eleanor Roosevelt.

faq.question: When did Franklin D. Roosevelt die?
faq.answer: He died on April 12, 1945.

timeline.event: Born as Marcus Aurelius Antoninus in Rome.
Adopted the dress and lifestyle of a philosopher on the orders of Diognetus.
Adopted by Hadrian's successor, Antoninus Pius.
Antoninus Pius ascended to the throne as emperor following Hadrian's death.
Served as consul under Antoninus Pius.
Became consul with Antoninus Pius as his co-consul.
Became consul for the second time.
Married Faustina, the daughter of Antoninus Pius.
Birth of his first daughter, Domitia Faustina.
Antoninus Pius granted Marcus tribunician power and imperium.
Faustina gave birth to twin brothers Titus Aurelius Antoninus and Tiberius Aelius Aurelius (who did not live long).
Birth of daughter Annia Aurelia Galeria Lucilla.
Estimated death of daughter Domitia Faustina.
Birth of son Tiberius Aelius Antoninus (who did not live long).
Death of Marcus's mother, Domitia Lucilla.
Faced another death of a child (name unknown).
Faustina gave birth to daughters Fadilla and Cornificia.
Following the death of Antoninus Pius, Marcus and Lucius Verus ascended as co-emperors.
Lowered the silver purity of the Roman denarius from 83.5% to 79%.
Vologases IV of Parthia entered the Kingdom of Armenia, expelled its king, and installed Pacorus.
Outbreak of the Parthian War.
Lucius left for the battlefield to personally handle the Parthian War.
Artaxata, the capital of Armenia, was captured.
Lucius gained the title 'Armeniacus'.
A new Armenian capital, Kaine Polis, replaced Artaxata.
The Roman army moved into Mesopotamia, captured Edessa, and reinstalled King Mannus. Nisibis fell.
Cassius's army reached Seleucia and Ctesiphon, captured Ctesiphon, burned the royal palace, and sacked Seleucia.
The Antonine Plague broke out in Mesopotamia and spread to Rome.
Cassius's army crossed the Tigris and headed into Media, returning to the battlefield. Lucius claimed the title 'Medicus'.
Marcus proclaimed his twin sons Annius and Commodus as heirs.
War broke out with Germanic tribes.
Revalued the denarius by increasing its silver purity from 79% to 82%.
Co-emperor Lucius likely died of the plague.
The Roman army routed the Germanic tribes in the province of Dacia, but commander Fronto was killed in action.
Moors in the North African provinces rebelled and invaded as far as Hispania.
Began co-ruling with his son Commodus.
Died at the age of 58 in a military camp near Vindobona or Sirmium.

keyAchievements.title: Emperor of the Roman Empire
keyAchievements.description: He was the 16th emperor of the Roman Empire (161-180), the last of the Five Good Emperors, and the last emperor of the Pax Romana.

keyAchievements.title: Stoic Philosopher
keyAchievements.description: As a Stoic philosopher, he wrote 'Meditations', leaving important material for understanding ancient Stoic philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Military victories
keyAchievements.description: Successfully fought wars in the East against the Parthian Empire and the Kingdom of Armenia, and subdued Germanic tribes in the Marcomannic Wars.

keyAchievements.title: Monetary reform
keyAchievements.description: Took measures to correct the silver purity of the Roman coin, the denarius.

keyAchievements.title: Legal and administrative activities
keyAchievements.description: Showed significant interest in three areas: the manumission of slaves, the protection of orphans and minors, and the choice of city councilors.

keyAchievements.title: Erection of monuments
keyAchievements.description: The Column of Marcus Aurelius and his equestrian statue were erected in Rome to commemorate his military victories.

faq.question: Who was Marcus Aurelius?
faq.answer: He was the 16th emperor of the Roman Empire (161-180) and a Stoic philosopher, being the last of the Five Good Emperors and the last emperor of the Pax Romana.

faq.question: What were his major military achievements?
faq.answer: He successfully fought wars in the East against the Parthian Empire and the Kingdom of Armenia, and subdued the Marcomanni, Quadi, and the Sarmatian Iazyges in the Marcomannic Wars.

faq.question: What is the important work he wrote?
faq.answer: The 'Meditations' he wrote is an important source for understanding ancient Stoic philosophy today and provides a window into his inner life.

faq.question: What difficulties did the Roman Empire face during his reign?
faq.answer: During his reign, the Roman Empire faced massive military conflicts, and the Antonine Plague that broke out in 165 or 166 devastated the Roman Empire's population, causing 5 million deaths out of a population of 10 million.

faq.question: How did Marcus Aurelius choose his successor?
faq.answer: He chose not to adopt an heir, and his son Commodus succeeded him as emperor, which was a subject of debate among both contemporary and modern historians.

timeline.event: Born in Ulm, in the Kingdom of Württemberg, German Empire.
Moved to Switzerland.
Renounced his German citizenship to avoid military service and enrolled in the mathematics and physics teaching diploma program at the Zurich Federal Polytechnic School.
Passed his mathematics and physics exams and received a federal teaching diploma.
Acquired Swiss citizenship.
Married Mileva Marić and secured a permanent position at the Swiss Patent Office in Bern.

Received his doctorate from the University of Zurich and published four groundbreaking papers on the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, special relativity, and mass-energy equivalence during his 'miracle year'.
Appointed as a lecturer at the University of Bern.
Appointed associate professor of theoretical physics at the University of Zurich.
Became a full professor at the German Charles-Ferdinand University in Prague.
Moved to Berlin to join the Prussian Academy of Sciences and Humboldt University.
Successfully completed the general theory of relativity.
Became director of the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Physics and became a German citizen again.
Married Elsa Löwenthal after divorcing Mileva Marić, and became world-famous after Sir Arthur Eddington confirmed his prediction of the deflection of light.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for his services to theoretical physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect.
Traveled to Asia, including Singapore, Ceylon, and Japan, as well as to Palestine.

Unable to return to Germany due to Adolf Hitler's rise to power while visiting the United States, he officially renounced his German citizenship and became a resident scholar at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton.
Signed a letter warning President Roosevelt of a potential German nuclear weapons program and recommending that the US begin similar research.
Became a US citizen.
Died at the age of 76.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Theory of Relativity
keyAchievements.description: Revolutionized our understanding of space, time, and gravity by developing the special and general theories of relativity, the two pillars of modern physics.

keyAchievements.title: Mass-Energy Equivalence Formula (E=mc²)
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the 'world's most famous equation', showing that mass and energy are interchangeable.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Law of the Photoelectric Effect
keyAchievements.description: Made a pivotal contribution to the development of quantum theory by proposing the concept that light acts as a stream of particles (photons), for which he won the Nobel Prize in Physics.

keyAchievements.title: Explanation of Brownian Motion
keyAchievements.description: Provided definitive evidence for the existence of atoms and molecules by explaining the random motion of particles suspended in a liquid.

keyAchievements.title: Laying the Foundation for Modern Cosmology
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for modern cosmology, the study of the structure and evolution of the universe based on general relativity.

keyAchievements.title: Prediction of Gravitational Waves
keyAchievements.description: Predicted the existence of gravitational waves—ripples in the curvature of spacetime propagating as waves—which were confirmed by observation nearly 100 years later.

faq.question: For which major achievement did Albert Einstein receive the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: He received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 'for his services to theoretical physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect'.

faq.question: What is Einstein's most famous scientific contribution?
faq.answer: He developed the theory of relativity and made significant contributions to the development of quantum mechanics, and his mass-energy equivalence formula E=mc² is known as the 'world's most famous equation'.

faq.question: Why did Einstein leave Germany and move to the United States?
faq.answer: When Adolf Hitler came to power in Germany in 1933, he settled in the United States in opposition to the Nazi government's policies, and became a US citizen in 1940.

faq.question: What was Einstein's stance on the development of nuclear weapons?
faq.answer: Just before World War II, he signed a letter warning President Roosevelt about the German nuclear weapons program, but he generally condemned the idea of nuclear weapons.

faq.question: What were Einstein's religious views?
faq.answer: He expressed sympathy for the impersonal pantheistic God of Baruch Spinoza's philosophy and called himself a 'deeply religious nonbeliever' rather than an atheist.

timeline.event: Born in Zundert, Netherlands.
His younger brother, Theodorus 'Theo', was born.
Was sent to the village school in Zundert.
Enrolled in a boarding school in Zevenbergen.
Was sent to a middle school in Tilburg.
Dropped out of school and returned home.
Found employment at the Goupil & Cie gallery in The Hague.
Transferred to the Goupil gallery's London branch on Southampton Street.
Was arranged a transfer to Paris.
Was dismissed from the Goupil gallery; worked as an unpaid supply teacher at a boarding school in Ramsgate, England; lived as an assistant to a Methodist minister.
Prepared for the theology entrance exam with his uncle, Johannes Stricker, in Amsterdam.
Failed the theology entrance exam and left his uncle's house; took a 3-month course at a Protestant missionary school in Laeken near Brussels.
Took up a post as a lay missionary in the mining village of Petit Wasmes in the Borinage, Belgium; was dismissed by the missionary organization.
Returned to Cuesmes and stayed with a miner; took up art in earnest at Theo's recommendation.
Left for Brussels and enrolled at the Académie Royale des Beaux-Arts.
Returned to Etten for a long stay with his family; proposed to his uncle's daughter, Cornelia 'Kee' Vos-Stricker, but was rejected.

Learned oil painting from Anton Mauve and set up a studio; began a relationship with Clasina Maria 'Sien' Hoornik, an alcoholic prostitute; spent three weeks in the hospital with an attack of gonorrhea.
Left Sien and her children; moved to Drenthe province in the northern Netherlands; returned to his parents' home in Nuenen.
Fell in love with his neighbor Margot Begemann, but she attempted suicide by poison due to their families' opposition to marriage.
His father died on March 26; completed 'The Potato Eaters'; 'The Potato Eaters' was first publicly exhibited in The Hague.
Moved to Antwerp.

Took the entrance exam for the higher level of the Royal Academy of Fine Arts in Antwerp and was admitted; was hospitalized due to overwork, poor diet, and excessive smoking; moved to Paris, lived in his brother Theo's apartment, and studied art at Fernand Cormon's studio.
Moved to Asnières-sur-Seine, a northwestern suburb of Paris; befriended Paul Gauguin; held an exhibition in Montmartre with Bernard, Anquetin, and Toulouse-Lautrec.

Left Paris for rest and moved to Arles in southern France; rented four rooms at 2 Place Lamartine in Arles; Paul Gauguin arrived in Arles; following an argument with Gauguin, he cut off his left ear and was hospitalized.

Returned to the Yellow House in Arles; a petition by townspeople led the police to close his house, sending him back to the hospital; voluntarily admitted himself to the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum in Saint-Rémy-de-Provence.

Albert Aurier praised his work in the 'Mercure de France'; received an invitation from Les XX in Brussels to participate in their annual exhibition; participated in the 6th Salon des Indépendants on the Champs-Élysées in Paris; left the clinic in Saint-Rémy and moved to Auvers-sur-Oise, a suburb of Paris.
Attempted suicide by shooting himself in the chest with a revolver on July 27.
Died on July 29.
Was buried in the municipal cemetery of Auvers-sur-Oise on July 30.
His brother Theo died on January 25.
Theo's widow, Johanna van Gogh-Bonger, had Theo's body exhumed and reburied next to Vincent's grave.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Post-Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: One of the most famous and influential Post-Impressionist painters in the history of Western art, contributing to the revival of Expressionism in modern art.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Artist
keyAchievements.description: Created about 2,100 artworks, including around 860 oil paintings, in just over a decade.

keyAchievements.title: Bold Colors and Brushwork
keyAchievements.description: Established a unique style characterized by bold colors and dramatic brushwork in landscapes, still lifes, portraits, and self-portraits.

keyAchievements.title: Symbolic Use of Color
keyAchievements.description: Believed that color carried psychological and moral weight beyond simple depiction, especially using yellow as a symbol of sunlight, life, and the divine.

keyAchievements.title: Self-Portrait Series
keyAchievements.description: Produced over 43 self-portraits between 1885 and 1889, expressing a high level of introspection and various mental and physical states.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Global Fame
keyAchievements.description: Though he sold only one painting during his lifetime, after his death, his art and life story captured the public imagination as a symbol of the misunderstood genius, and his works are among the most expensive paintings ever sold.

faq.question: How many paintings did Vincent van Gogh sell during his lifetime?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, Van Gogh sold only one painting: 'The Red Vineyard at Arles'.

faq.question: How many artworks did Vincent van Gogh create in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He created about 2,100 artworks, including around 860 oil paintings, in just over a decade.

faq.question: Why did Vincent van Gogh cut off his ear?
faq.answer: Following an argument with Paul Gauguin, he seemingly hallucinated and severed all or part of his left ear with a razor, indicating that he suffered an acute mental breakdown.

faq.question: How did Vincent van Gogh die?
faq.answer: On July 27, 1890, at the age of 37, he attempted suicide by shooting himself in the chest with a revolver in a wheat field, and died of an infection from the wound two days later on July 29.

faq.question: What was the relationship like between Vincent van Gogh and his brother Theo?
faq.answer: His younger brother Theo supported Vincent financially and emotionally, and the two maintained a deep friendship, exchanging hundreds of letters throughout their lives.

faq.question: Which of Van Gogh's paintings sold for the highest price?
faq.answer: On May 15, 1990, his 'Portrait of Dr. Gachet' (first version) sold for $82.5 million at Christie's, setting a new record high price.

timeline.event: Born on April 28 (March 8 by the lunar calendar) as the third son of Yi Jeong in Geoncheon-dong, Southern Ward, Hanseong-bu.
Married Lady Bang and prepared for the military examination while studying military arts under the sponsorship of his father-in-law, Bang Jin.
Took the special military examination at the age of 27, but fell from his horse and failed.

Passed the triennial military examination on March 22 at the age of 32, placing fourth in the Pyonggwa (丙科) category, and began his official career as an acting instructor (Gwonji Hullyeonwon Bongsa).
Became the border defense commander (Gwongwan) of Donggubibo in December.
Served as a military training official (Hullyeonwon Bongsa) in Haemi for 10 months.
Transferred in December to serve as a military officer for the Army Commander of Chungcheong Province.
Transferred in July to serve as the naval commander (Manho) of Balpojin in Jeolla Province.
While serving as the commander of Balpojin in January, he was dismissed by Seo Ik for allegedly failing to properly maintain military weapons, but was soon reinstated.
Became a military training official (Hullyeonwon Bongsa) in May.
Recommended by Yi Yong, Army Commander of South Hamgyong Province, in July, and became his military officer.
Recommended and sent out as the border defense commander (Gwongwan) of Geonwonbo in August.
Suffered the death of his father on November 15.
Became an assistant master (Jubu) in the Royal Stables in January, followed by concurrent appointments as commander (Manho) of Josan and military farming supervisor (Dunjeonsaui) of Nokdo.
In September, the Battle of Nokdundo broke out following a surprise attack by the Jurchens, and Yi Sun-shin stayed behind with Yi Gyeong-rok to fight and achieve victory.
In October, the Army Commander of the North, Yi Il, blamed him for the defeat at Nokdundo, resulting in his dismissal, imprisonment, and conscription into the army as a commoner (Baeguijonggun).
Was pardoned and reinstated after capturing the Jurchen leader Ueulginae during the second expedition to Nokdundo.
Recommended by Yi San-hae, Jeong Eon-sin, and others for exceptional appointment by the Border Defense Council (Bibyeonsa) in January.
Became a military officer for the Inspector-General of Jeolla Province in February.
Became a military royal messenger (Mugyeom Seonjeongwan) in November.
Appointed as the magistrate of Jeongeup in December upon the recommendation of Ryu Seong-ryong, and governed the town with benevolent administration, receiving widespread praise from the people.

By order of King Seonjo in February, he was promoted from magistrate of Jeongeup to county magistrate of Jindo, commander of Garipo, and then appointed as the Naval Commander of the Left Jeolla Province.
Received news of the Japanese invasion at Busanpo through a dispatch from Won Gyun at 10 PM on May 26 (April 16 by the lunar calendar).
Achieved his first victory at the Battle of Okpo on June 16 (May 7 by the lunar calendar), sinking 26 Japanese fleet ships.
Deployed the Turtle Ship for the first time at the Battle of Sacheon on July 8 (May 29 by the lunar calendar), destroying 13 Japanese ships.
Severely defeated the Japanese forces using the 'Crane Wing' formation (Hakikjin) at the Battle of Hansan Island on August 14 (July 8 by the lunar calendar).
Promoted to the rank of Jaheondaebu on August 16.
Destroyed over 100 Japanese ships at the Battle of Busan on September 1 (lunar calendar).
Advanced to Ungpo in Ungcheon on March 12 (February 10 by the lunar calendar) and engaged in the Battle of Ungpo.
Appointed by the Joseon royal court as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces (Samdo Sugun Tongjesa) on August 1 (lunar calendar).
Dismissed from the post of Commander-in-Chief on April 11 (February 25 by the lunar calendar), escorted to Hanseong, and imprisoned.
Spared from the death penalty on May 16 (April 1 by the lunar calendar) and ordered to serve as a common soldier (Baeguijonggun) under Commander-in-Chief Gwon Yul.
His mother passed away on a boat on May 26 (April 11 by the lunar calendar) while he was serving as a common soldier.
Reappointed as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces on August 28 (July 16 by the lunar calendar) after Won Gyun's devastating defeat at the Battle of Chilcheollyang.
Achieved a great victory at the Battle of Myeongnyang on October 26 (September 16 by the lunar calendar), destroying over 130 of the hundreds of Japanese ships with only 13 warships.
Killed in action by a stray bullet while pursuing retreating Japanese forces at the Battle of Noryang on November 19 (lunar calendar).
Posthumously promoted to Senior First Rank (Jeong 1-pum), Daegwang Boguk Sungnok Daebu, Right State Councilor of the State Council, and concurrent Royal Lecturer in December.
Posthumously honored as a First Class Seonmu Merit Subject and Deokpung Buwongun in June, and additionally granted the title of Left State Councilor and concurrent Royal Lecturer in July.
Received the posthumous title 'Chungmu' (Loyalty and Valor) from King Injo in March, becoming Chungmugong.
Additionally posthumously promoted to Senior First Rank, Chief State Councilor of the State Council, by King Jeongjo in July.

keyAchievements.title: Passed the Military Examination
keyAchievements.description: Passed the triennial military examination in 1576 at the age of 32, placing fourth in the Pyonggwa category, and began his official career.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the Battle of Nokdundo
keyAchievements.description: In 1587, during the Battle of Nokdundo caused by a surprise attack by Jurchens, he stayed behind with Yi Gyeong-rok, fought and won, rescuing over 60 Joseon citizens.

keyAchievements.title: Development and Construction of the Turtle Ship
keyAchievements.description: Immediately after his appointment as Left Naval Commander of Jeolla, he strengthened military preparations by expanding weapons and provisions and developing and building the Turtle Ship in preparation for war.

keyAchievements.title: Victories in Major Naval Battles of the Imjin War
keyAchievements.description: After the outbreak of the Imjin War, he successively defeated the Japanese navy in major naval battles including Okpo, Sacheon, Hansan Island, and Busan, seizing control of the sea and cutting off Japanese supply lines.

keyAchievements.title: Served as Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces
keyAchievements.description: Appointed as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces (Samdo Sugun Tongjesa) in 1593, taking supreme command and rebuilding the Joseon navy.

keyAchievements.title: Great Victory at the Battle of Myeongnyang
keyAchievements.description: After the defeat at Chilcheollyang, he achieved a great victory in the Myeongnyang Strait with 12 ships against hundreds of Japanese ships, reversing the tide of the Jeongyu War (Second Invasion).

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Noryang and Death in Action
keyAchievements.description: In the Battle of Noryang in 1598, he joined forces with the Ming navy to heavily defeat the retreating Japanese forces, and was killed in action by a stray bullet during the battle.

keyAchievements.title: Revered as a Hero-Saint
keyAchievements.description: Revered as a martial saint who saved the nation through successive victories in naval battles against the Japanese navy with brilliant intellect and outstanding strategy as the Commander-in-Chief of the Naval Forces of the Three Provinces during the Imjin War.

faq.question: When and where was Yi Sun-shin born?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin was born on April 28, 1545 (March 8 by the lunar calendar) in Geoncheon-dong, Southern Ward, Hanseong-bu (near the current Sindo Building at 19 Eulji-ro 18-gil, Jung-gu, Seoul).

faq.question: When did Yi Sun-shin pass the military examination?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin passed the triennial military examination on March 22, 1576 (the 9th year of King Seonjo) at the age of 32, placing fourth in the Pyonggwa category.

faq.question: Why was Yi Sun-shin stripped of his rank and made to serve as a common soldier (Baeguijonggun) twice?
faq.answer: The first time was in 1587 when the Northern Army Commander Yi Il wrongly blamed him for the defeat at the Battle of Nokdundo, leading to his imprisonment and subsequent conscription as a common soldier. The second time was in 1597 when he was dismissed from his post as Commander-in-Chief and ordered to serve as a common soldier on charges of disobeying the court's orders.

faq.question: In which naval battle did Yi Sun-shin die in action?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin was killed in action by a stray bullet on November 19, 1598 (lunar calendar), during the Battle of Noryang while pursuing the retreating Japanese forces.

faq.question: What are the famous writings left by Yi Sun-shin?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin left behind 'Nanjung Ilgi' (War Diary), which records his experiences during the Imjin War, and 'Chungmugong Yi Sun-shin Jeonseo', a complete collection of his posthumous works.

faq.question: What posthumous title did Yi Sun-shin receive?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin received the posthumous title 'Chungmu' (Loyalty and Valor) from King Injo in March 1643 (the 21st year of King Injo), thus becoming Chungmugong.

faq.question: What was Yi Sun-shin's family structure?
faq.answer: Yi Sun-shin had three sons (Yi Hoe, Yi Ye, Yi Myeon) and one daughter with his wife, Lady Bang of Sangju (Onyang Bang clan), and two sons (Yi Hun, Yi Sin) and two daughters with his concubine, Lady Oh of Haeju.

timeline.event: Birth of Gwanggaeto the Great, the 19th monarch of Goguryeo.
Accession of his father, King Gogugyang, following the death of his uncle, King Sosurim.
Invested as Crown Prince.
Ascended to the throne as the 19th monarch of Goguryeo following King Gogugyang, and began using the era name Yeongnak.
Led 40,000 troops to attack the northern border of Baekje, capturing over 10 fortresses including Seokhyeonseong.
When Baekje attacked Goguryeo's southern border, ordered his generals to repel them.
Conquered the Khitan, recovering over 10,000 people who had been taken captive in the 8th year of King Sosurim (378).
Captured Gwanmiseong, a northern stronghold of Baekje, after a 20-day siege.
Death of King Jinsa of Baekje and accession of King Asin.
Repelled a Baekje attack at Gwanmiseong. Founded 9 Buddhist temples in Pyongyang.
Repelled a Baekje attack at Sugokseong. Built 7 fortresses on the southern border to defend against Baekje invasions.
Captured over 8,000 Baekje troops alive and achieved a great victory at Paesu. Advanced to Yeomsu (Salt Water), conquered and subjugated the Khitan tribe Paeryeo.

Launched a massive attack on Baekje, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and laid siege to Wiryeseong. Received the surrender of King Asin of Baekje and returned triumphantly, taking the king's brother and 10 ministers as hostages.
Conquered the Sushen to stabilize the northeastern border region.
Conducted a royal tour to Pyongyang. When Baekje and Wa (Japan) attacked Silla, Silla sent envoys to Pyongyang to plead for help.

Dispatched a large army of 50,000 to Silla to defeat the Wa forces. Pursued and repelled the Wa forces as far as Jongbalseong in Geumgwan Gaya. Replaced the king of Silla from Naemul Maripgan to Silseong Maripgan. King Murong Sheng of Later Yan attacked Sinseong and Namsosŏng.
Attacked and captured Sukgunseong of Later Yan.
Annihilated Wa forces that invaded the Daifang region. Invaded Later Yan and attacked as far as Yan Commandery, capturing it.
Defeated the Later Yan army at Yodongseong (Liaodong Fortress).
Defeated the Later Yan army at Mokjeoseong, firmly securing the occupation of Liaodong.
Mobilized 50,000 men to defeat the Later Yan army and captured massive spoils of war. Captured 6 fortresses of Later Yan on the way back.
When Go Un, who was of Goguryeo descent, founded Northern Yan, he established friendly relations, thereby stabilizing the western border.
Built 6 fortresses including Doksanseong in the east of the country and relocated households from Pyongyang to live there. Conducted a royal tour to the south.
Attacked and subjugated Eastern Buyeo.
Passed away.
King Jangsu erected a stele at his tomb and, in accordance with his will, appointed tomb keepers to manage the royal mausoleum.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Baekje
keyAchievements.description: Launched a massive attack on Baekje in 396, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and receiving the surrender of King Asin.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Khitan and Sushen
keyAchievements.description: Conquered the Khitan in 392 to recover captured subjects, and conquered the Sushen in 398 to stabilize the northeastern border region.

keyAchievements.title: Rescue of Silla and Defeat of Wa
keyAchievements.description: Dispatched an army of 50,000 to Silla in 400 to defeat the allied forces of Baekje and Wa, and established influence over Silla by doing things such as replacing its king.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in War Against Later Yan
keyAchievements.description: Secured the occupation of Liaodong by winning fierce wars against Later Yan, and stabilized the western border through friendly relations with Northern Yan.

keyAchievements.title: Subjugation of Eastern Buyeo
keyAchievements.description: Strengthened Goguryeo's northern influence by attacking and subjugating Eastern Buyeo in 410.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening Internal Affairs
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of the state by emphasizing Pyongyang, building 9 temples and fortresses there, and establishing a system for maintaining previous royal tombs and tomb keepers.

faq.question: What was the name of Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: His name was Damdeok or An.

faq.question: Which monarch of Goguryeo was Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: He was the 19th monarch of Goguryeo.

faq.question: When did Gwanggaeto the Great reign?
faq.answer: He reigned from the 5th lunar month of 391 to the 10th lunar month of 412.

faq.question: What was the era name of Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: His era name was Yeongnak.

faq.question: What results did Gwanggaeto the Great achieve in the war against Baekje?
faq.answer: In 396, he launched a massive attack on Baekje, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and received the surrender of King Asin of Baekje.

faq.question: How did Gwanggaeto the Great rescue Silla?
faq.answer: In 400, he dispatched an army of 50,000 to Silla, defeated the allied forces of Baekje and Wa, and exerted influence over Silla by replacing its king.

faq.question: When was the Gwanggaeto Stele erected?
faq.answer: The Stele of Gwanggaeto the Great was erected by King Jangsu in 414.

timeline.event: Born in the State of Zhao to Prince Yiren and Zhao Ji
Began reign as the 31st King of the State of Qin (Lü Buwei began regency)
Began personal rule
Conquered the State of Han
Conquered the State of Zhao
Conquered the State of Wei
Conquered the State of Chu
Conquered the State of Yan
Conquered the State of Qi and unified the Chinese mainland
Carried out the Burning of Books (Fen Shu)
Carried out the Burying of Scholars (Keng Ru)
Died at the age of 50 in Shaqiu during his final inspection tour

keyAchievements.title: Unification of China
keyAchievements.description: Unified a divided China and established the imperial system and the commandery-county system (Junxian system), laying the basic framework for Chinese dynasties over the next 2,000 years.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Imperial System
keyAchievements.description: First to use the title 'Emperor' (Huangdi) instead of 'King' and proclaimed himself 'First Emperor' (Shi Huangdi).

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of the Commandery-County System
keyAchievements.description: Following the advice of Chancellor Li Si, he divided the entire country into 36 commanderies and appointed an administrator, a military commander, and an inspector to each, establishing a centralized system of governance.

keyAchievements.title: Standardization of Weights, Measures, Currency, and Script
keyAchievements.description: To rule the empire efficiently, he standardized weights and measures, currency, and writing systems, and improved the road networks.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the Great Wall
keyAchievements.description: Connected the defensive walls of the Warring States period to complete the Great Wall to prevent invasions by the Xiongnu.

faq.question: When was Qin Shi Huang born?
faq.answer: Qin Shi Huang was born in 259 BC in the State of Zhao to Prince Yiren, who was sent there as a hostage from the State of Qin, and Zhao Ji.

faq.question: Why did Qin Shi Huang use the title 'Emperor' (Huangdi)?
faq.answer: Considering the title of 'King' inadequate for himself, Qin Shi Huang took the characters 'Huang' and 'Di' from the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors to form 'Huangdi' (Emperor), and styled himself the 'First Emperor' (Shi Huangdi).

faq.question: What were the major tyrannical acts of Qin Shi Huang?
faq.answer: Qin Shi Huang carried out the 'Burning of Books and Burying of Scholars', a massive cultural suppression in which he burned all books except those concerning the history of Qin, medicine, and agriculture, and buried alive over 460 scholars.

faq.question: What efforts did Qin Shi Huang make to achieve immortality?
faq.answer: In his quest for the elixir of life, Qin Shi Huang sent the alchemist Xu Fu with 3,000 boys and girls and a ship laden with treasures to Mount Penglai. He also ingested mercury, mistakenly believing it to be the elixir of immortality.

faq.question: How did Qin Shi Huang die?
faq.answer: During his final inspection tour in 210 BC, Qin Shi Huang fell severely ill in the region of Shaqiu and passed away at the age of 50.

timeline.event: Born in the city of Rome.
Death of his father, Octavius.
Delivered the funeral oration for his maternal grandmother, Julia Caesaris.
Donned the toga virilis and was elected to the College of Pontiffs.
Presided over the Greek games in honor of the Temple of Venus Genetrix, built by Caesar.
Assassination of his adoptive father, Julius Caesar.
Arrived in Rome and met Mark Antony.
Defeated two legions under Antony's command.
Admitted to the Senate and granted military command.
Defeated Antony's forces at the Battle of Forum Gallorum and the Battle of Mutina.
Appointed consul.
Formed the Second Triumvirate with Antony and Lepidus near Bologna.
The Roman Senate declared Julius Caesar a god.
Defeated the forces of Brutus and Cassius at the Battle of Philippi.
Ended the siege of Lucius Antonius at Perusia and received his surrender.
Executed 300 senators and equestrians who had collaborated with Lucius Antonius.
Signed the Treaty of Brundisium with Antony.
Married his older sister, Octavia Minor, to Antony.
Concluded a temporary peace agreement, the Pact of Misenum, with Sextus Pompey.
Divorced Scribonia and married Livia Drusilla.
Agreed to extend the Triumvirate for another five years.
Completely destroyed Sextus Pompey's fleet at the Battle of Naulochus.
The Senate stripped Antony of his consular powers and declared war on Egypt.
Defeated the fleets of Antony and Cleopatra at the Battle of Actium.
Began holding the consulship every year.
Achieved victory in Alexandria, leading to the suicides of Antony and Cleopatra.
Granted the title of 'First Citizen of the State' (princeps civitatis) by the Senate.
After ending the civil wars, declared the return of extraordinary powers to the Senate and was awarded the title 'Augustus'.
Married his daughter Julia to his sister's son, Marcellus.
Marcellus died, and Augustus resigned from the consulship.
Personally undertook the construction of roads.
Finally suppressed the rebellion in the Cantabria region of Spain.
Assumed the office of Pontifex Maximus after the death of Lepidus.
His stepson Drusus died after falling from a horse in Germania.
Elected consul, and his maternal grandson Gaius Caesar began his political career.
Elected consul, his maternal grandson Lucius Caesar began his political career, and he was granted the title of 'Father of the Country' (pater patriae) by the Senate and the Roman people.
His maternal grandson Lucius Caesar died young.
His maternal grandson Gaius Caesar died young, and he adopted Tiberius and Agrippa Postumus as his sons.
Established the 'Military Treasury' (aerarium militare) to fund pensions for active and retired soldiers.
Three Roman legions were annihilated by Germanic tribes in the Teutoburg Forest.
Tiberius was granted all the prerogatives held by Augustus.
Died in Nola, and the Senate and the popular assembly declared him a god.

keyAchievements.title: First Emperor of the Roman Empire
keyAchievements.description: Reigned as the first Emperor of the Roman Empire and the founder of the Julio-Claudian dynasty.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Pax Romana
keyAchievements.description: His rule ushered in an era of peace and prosperity known as the 'Pax Romana' (Roman Peace), which sustained peace in the Mediterranean world for over two centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of Imperial Territory and Border Protection
keyAchievements.description: Expanded the territory of the Roman Empire, protected its borders and allies, and concluded a peace treaty with Parthia.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of the State System
keyAchievements.description: Reformed Rome's taxation system, built an overland transportation network, and established a standing army, a small navy, and the Praetorian Guard.

keyAchievements.title: Redevelopment of the City of Rome
keyAchievements.description: Established police and fire-fighting forces in Rome and extensively redeveloped large parts of the city.

keyAchievements.title: Recording the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti'
keyAchievements.description: Before his death, he left behind a record of his accomplishments known as the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti'.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Title 'Augustus'
keyAchievements.description: Received the title 'Augustus', meaning 'the revered one', from the Senate.

keyAchievements.title: Granted the Title 'First Citizen of the State'
keyAchievements.description: Was granted the title 'princeps civitatis' (First Citizen of the State) by the Senate, meaning the leading figure.

keyAchievements.title: Securing Military Command
keyAchievements.description: Became the supreme commander of the Roman military and held the authority to celebrate triumphs.

keyAchievements.title: Tax Reform
keyAchievements.description: Executed tax reforms, including a fixed-rate direct tax on provincials and the introduction of a tax collection system managed by civil servants.

keyAchievements.title: Naming of the Month 'August'
keyAchievements.description: The former 'sixth month' (Sextilis) was renamed 'Augustus' (August) in his honor.

keyAchievements.title: Construction Projects
keyAchievements.description: Contributed so significantly to the architectural development of Rome that he famously boasted, 'I found Rome a city of bricks and left it a city of marble'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Military Pension Fund
keyAchievements.description: Established the 'Military Treasury' (aerarium militare) to provide pensions for active duty and retired soldiers.

faq.question: What was the real name of Augustus?
faq.answer: Before being adopted by Caesar, his name was Gaius Octavius, and after adoption, he became known as Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus.

faq.question: How did Augustus become the first emperor of the Roman Empire?
faq.answer: After the collapse of the Second Triumvirate, he monopolized power and, upon receiving the title 'Augustus' from the Senate in 27 BC, became the first emperor of the Roman Empire.

faq.question: What was the greatest achievement of the Augustan era?
faq.answer: His rule brought about an era of prosperity known as the 'Pax Romana' (Roman Peace), sustaining peace in the Mediterranean world for more than two centuries.

faq.question: Did Augustus enjoy gambling?
faq.answer: Contrary to his stoic image, he enjoyed gambling, particularly dice and odd-even games, and often built friendships by intentionally losing money.

faq.question: Why was the month of August named after Augustus?
faq.answer: By decision of the Roman Senate, the month was named in his honor to commemorate his capture of Alexandria and ascent to power in August of 30 BC.

timeline.event: Born in Schönhausen, Prussia
Enrolled at the Plamannsche Lehranstalt in Berlin
Enrolled in law at the University of Göttingen
Began practical training as a legal clerk after graduating from university
Began military service
Started life as a farm manager after completing military service
Entered politics by being elected as a member of the United Diet of Saxony
Acted as a counter-revolutionary during the March Revolution in Berlin
Elected as a member of the Prussian United Diet
Appointed as the Prussian envoy to the Federal Diet in Frankfurt
Appointed as the Prussian ambassador to Russia
Appointed Minister President of Prussia after serving as ambassador to France
Delivered the 'Blood and Iron' speech in parliament
Victory in the Second Schleswig War against Denmark
Victory in the Austro-Prussian War and signing of the Peace of Prague
Formation of the North German Confederation
Victory in the Franco-Prussian War
Proclamation of the German Empire and appointment as its first Chancellor
Began the 'Kulturkampf' (culture struggle) suppressing Catholics in southern Germany
Establishment of the Reichsbank (Imperial Bank)
Presided over the Congress of Berlin following the Russo-Turkish War
Enacted the 'Anti-Socialist Laws'
Implemented health insurance
Implemented accident insurance and acquired colonies in Togo and Cameroon, Africa
Acquired the colony of German East Africa
Implemented old age and disability insurance
Resigned and retired from politics due to policy conflicts with Wilhelm II
Death

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Germany and Creation of the German Empire
keyAchievements.description: Achieved the unification of Germany and established the German Empire by expanding Prussia's military and winning wars against Denmark, Austria, and France.

keyAchievements.title: Blood and Iron Policy
keyAchievements.description: Earned the nickname 'Iron Chancellor' through a speech advocating military expansion upon taking office, using it as a basis to strengthen military power.

keyAchievements.title: Maintaining the Balance of Power in Europe
keyAchievements.description: As Chancellor of the German Empire, he dominated European diplomacy and strove to maintain a balance of power among the great powers by forging agreements such as the League of the Three Emperors and the Triple Alliance.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Social Security Systems
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for a welfare state by implementing the world's first social security systems, including health insurance, accident insurance, and old age and disability insurance.

keyAchievements.title: Acquisition of Colonies
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to securing German colonies in Africa, such as Togo, Cameroon, and German East Africa, by encouraging the development of German capitalism and colonial expansion.

faq.question: Why is Bismarck called the 'Iron Chancellor'?
faq.answer: He earned this nickname from his 'Blood and Iron' speech in 1862, which advocated military expansion, though he shifted to a pacifist policy after unification.

faq.question: How did Bismarck achieve the unification of Germany?
faq.answer: Following his 'Blood and Iron' policy, he expanded the military and won wars against Denmark, Austria, and France, leading to the proclamation of the German Empire and achieving unification.

faq.question: What were Bismarck's major domestic policies?
faq.answer: He implemented the 'Kulturkampf' to suppress Catholics, enacted the 'Anti-Socialist Laws' to suppress socialism, and introduced the world's first social security systems.

faq.question: Why did Bismarck resign from the chancellorship?
faq.answer: He resigned and retired from politics in 1890 due to policy conflicts with Emperor Wilhelm II.

timeline.event: Born as the third son of Tsar Alexis Mikhailovich and his second wife, Natalya Kirillovna Naryshkina.

Ascended to the throne after the early death of his older half-brother Fyodor III, but lost power due to a coup by his older half-sister Princess Sophia, and spent his childhood and youth in the Foreign Quarter.
Married Eudoxia Lopukhina, daughter of a Russian aristocrat.
Taking advantage of the aristocracy's withdrawal of support for Sophia's regime, he staged a coup, exiled Sophia to a convent, and seized control of state affairs.
Participated in the resumed Azov campaigns against the Ottoman Empire as an artilleryman.
Organized a navy and attacked Azov again, easily capturing it.
Following the death of his half-brother Ivan V, he became the sole autocrat of the Tsardom of Russia.
Joined an alliance with Denmark and Poland against Charles XII of Sweden and entered the Great Northern War.
Began the construction of the new capital, St. Petersburg, on the barren coastal lands of the Baltic Sea acquired as a result of the Northern War.
Inflicted a decisive defeat on the Swedish army, personally commanded by Charles XII, at Poltava.
The Ottoman Empire declared war on the Tsardom of Russia after Charles XII formed an alliance with them.
When surrounded by Ottoman forces along the Pruth River, he surrendered to minimize casualties, giving up Azov and the Black Sea fleet in exchange.
Defeated the Swedish fleet at the Battle of Gangut.
His arch-rival Charles XII died in battle without an heir.
His only son, Tsarevich Alexei Petrovich, died in prison from the aftereffects of torture.
The Great Northern War concluded with a Russian victory, the Tsardom of Russia was proclaimed the Russian Empire, and Peter was given the title of Imperator.
While inspecting the construction site of St. Petersburg, he jumped into the water to save a drowning soldier and contracted pneumonia.
Died from complications of pneumonia.

keyAchievements.title: Launch and Development of the Russian Empire
keyAchievements.description: He advanced the Tsardom of Russia through Westernization policies and territorial expansion, and launched the Russian Empire by adopting the title of Imperator.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of Westernization Policies
keyAchievements.description: To modernize Russia, which still bore strong remnants of the Mongol Empire, he promoted sweeping Westernization policies, including changes to women's clothing, a beard tax, and administrative reform.

keyAchievements.title: Creation and Strengthening of the Navy
keyAchievements.description: Realizing the need for a fleet during the Azov campaigns, he organized a powerful navy that contributed decisively to the capture of Azov and the victory in the Great Northern War.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the Great Northern War
keyAchievements.description: He secured trade routes to the Baltic Sea by winning the Great Northern War against Sweden, transforming Russia into a major power on the Baltic coast.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of St. Petersburg
keyAchievements.description: Built the new capital, St. Petersburg, on the barren lands of the Baltic Sea acquired from the Northern War, creating a city that symbolized Russia's Westernization.

keyAchievements.title: Administrative and Academic Reforms
keyAchievements.description: Created permanent administrative bodies modeled after Prussia, established the Table of Ranks, simplified the Cyrillic alphabet, and encouraged scholarship by founding academic institutions.

faq.question: Why did Peter I promote Westernization policies?
faq.answer: He prioritized Westernization policies to modernize Russia, which lagged behind Western Europe in development and retained strong remnants of the Mongol Empire.

faq.question: How did Peter I come to create a navy?
faq.answer: Experiencing difficulties in besieging fortresses without a fleet during the Azov campaigns against the Ottoman Empire, he pursued the construction of a fleet using knowledge he had acquired since childhood.

faq.question: What happened to Peter I's son?
faq.answer: His only son, Tsarevich Alexei Petrovich, opposed his father's Westernization policies and attempted to orchestrate an assassination. Upon being discovered, he fled into exile but eventually returned, and died in prison from the aftereffects of torture.

faq.question: How did Peter I die?
faq.answer: In December 1724, while inspecting the construction site of St. Petersburg, he jumped into the water to save a drowning soldier. This caused him to contract pneumonia, leading to his death on February 8 of the following year.

timeline.event: Born in the vilayet of Salonica (now Thessaloniki, Greece) under the rule of the Ottoman Empire.
Enrolled in the Salonica Military School.
Enrolled in the Manastir Military High School.
Enrolled in the Ottoman Military Academy.
Graduated from the Ottoman Military Academy.
Graduated from the Ottoman Military College.
Promoted to Senior Captain.
Assigned to the Third Army headquarters in Manastir.
Appointed as the Ottoman railway inspector of Eastern Rumelia.
Joined the Young Turk Revolution.
Returned to Istanbul after suppressing a tribal rebellion.
Contributed to the suppression of the 31 March Incident, a counter-coup in Istanbul.
Assigned to Ottoman Albania.
Participated in large-scale military maneuvers in Picardy, France, as an Ottoman observer officer.
Briefly served in the Ottoman Ministry of War in Istanbul.
Deployed to Ottoman Tripolitania (modern-day Libya) and fought in the Italo-Turkish War.
Achieved a major victory in the Battle of Tobruk.
Suffered vision impairment in his left eye due to an injury sustained at the Battle of Derna.
Appointed commander of the Ottoman forces at Derna.
Returned from the Italo-Turkish War front upon the outbreak of the Balkan Wars.
The Italo-Turkish War ended, and under the Treaty of Ouchy, Ottoman Tripolitania and other territories were ceded to the Kingdom of Italy.
Achieved a decisive victory in the Battle of Sakarya, putting an end to the Megali Idea (Greater Greece).
The date of the great victory against Greece, which is celebrated as a national holiday in Turkey.
Abolished the Sultanate and deposed Mehmed VI.
Signed a new treaty (the Treaty of Lausanne) with the Allied Powers.
Moved the capital from Istanbul to Ankara, proclaimed the republic, and became the first President.
Abolished the Caliphate, an institution that had existed for 1,300 years since 632.
Implemented dress reforms to emancipate women's attire, instituted co-educational equality, and replaced the Islamic calendar with the European Gregorian calendar.
Amended the civil code to ban polygamy and establish monogamy.
Abolished the Arabic script for the Turkish language and replaced it with a Latin-based alphabet.
Granted voting rights to women.
The Turkish parliament bestowed upon him the surname 'Atatürk', meaning 'Father of the Turks'.
Signs of Atatürk's deteriorating health began to appear.
Suffered from severe illness during a trip to Yalova and was diagnosed with cirrhosis of the liver.
Died at the age of 57 at the Dolmabahçe Palace.

keyAchievements.title: Founder and First President of the Republic of Turkey
keyAchievements.description: As the founder and first President of the Republic of Turkey, he led the Turkish War of Independence.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the Battle of Gallipoli
keyAchievements.description: An outstanding general who successfully halted the offensive of the British and French forces at the Battle of Gallipoli during World War I.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the Sultanate and Caliphate
keyAchievements.description: Abolished the Sultanate in 1922, deposing Mehmed VI, and abolished the Caliphate in 1924.

keyAchievements.title: Legislation of Secularism
keyAchievements.description: Enshrined secularism, the founding principle of the Republic of Turkey, into law.

keyAchievements.title: Dress Reforms and Ensuring Women's Right to Education
keyAchievements.description: Enacted dress reforms to emancipate women's attire and guaranteed the previously forbidden right of women to an education, instituting co-educational equality.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the Islamic Calendar and Introduction of the Gregorian Calendar
keyAchievements.description: Replaced the Islamic calendar with the European Gregorian calendar.

keyAchievements.title: Revision of the Civil Code and Establishment of Monogamy
keyAchievements.description: Amended the civil code to prohibit polygamy and establish monogamy.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Latin Alphabet
keyAchievements.description: Abolished the Arabic script used for writing Turkish and replaced it with a Latin-based alphabet.

keyAchievements.title: Granting Women the Right to Vote
keyAchievements.description: Granted women the right to vote in 1930.

faq.question: What was Atatürk's original name?
faq.answer: His childhood name was Mustafa. The name 'Kemal' was given to him by his mathematics teacher, who praised his mathematical abilities and mature demeanor.

faq.question: What does the title 'Atatürk' mean and when was it bestowed?
